summary: Asparouhova argues that digital communities and online spaces remain largely unmapped, unlike our physical world where Google Maps has completed centuries of cartographic work. She draws parallels between historical mapmaking and the need to chart digital territories, noting that maps both empower and expose communities. She outlines her methodology for mapping digital worlds—a process of echolocation-like sensing, recording landmarks, and iterative refinement—and surveys various mapping techniques from skeumorphic to algorithmic approaches. She concludes that mapping digital spaces could legitimize them and help reduce misunderstandings when online ideas enter mainstream discourse, even though mapping also creates vulnerability.
key_points: Digital communities remain in an 'IYKYK' unmapped state, causing outsiders to misinterpret them using existing but ill-fitting frameworks, as seen with media coverage of effective altruism

Mapping digital territories is fundamentally different from physical cartography—it requires 'echolocation' and proprioceptive sensing rather than direct observation, and the results are more subjective and ephemeral

The most useful digital maps focus on people and tribes rather than outputs or technologies, because the number of influential people is far smaller and more stable than the constantly changing outputs they produce
Different mapping methodologies (skeumorphic, type-of-guy, schematic, matrix, word cloud) each have distinct tradeoffs between breadth and depth of information conveyed
Mapping digital worlds creates power dynamics similar to physical cartography—it can legitimize communities but also expose them to outside pressure and conquest
money_quotes: We are still in this sort of uncharted, 'natives only,' IYKYK stage of cartography in the digital world.
People who are deep in a particular world often underestimate the value of their own tacit knowledge.
When outsiders do stumble across your digital land, and they don't have a map, they start trying to fit it into the frameworks they do have, and things get very confusing quickly.
Mapping digital territories is more like echolocation, where you're standing in a dark space, totally blind, and you need to 'ping' the space around you and see what you get back.

If there are, say, a million points of output you could be looking at, the number of people creating them is more like a hundred data points. And that's a much easier universe to wrap our heads around.
Maybe digital spaces are still in this ephemeral, nomadic tribe state because we artificially keep them there by refusing to map them.
If we want to take our digital worlds more seriously, part of that starts with finding ways to legitimize them, instead of leaving them undefined.
key_insight: Digital communities remain unmapped and therefore misunderstood by outsiders, and developing rigorous cartographic practices for online spaces could legitimize them and reduce the distortions that occur when digital ideas enter mainstream discourse.
